title: Bangladeshi Chicken Roast

ingredients.name: chicken, large pieces with bone (legs, thighs)
visual_checks: Bone-in pieces are essential — they release gelatin that thickens the gravy.
ingredients.notes: Bone-in pieces are essential — they release gelatin that thickens the gravy.

subs.name: 800 g bone-in mutton pieces — increase the braising time to 40 minutes and add 100 ml extra water.

ingredients.name: onion, thinly sliced (for beresta and curry)
visual_checks: Fry 2 onions into golden beresta (fried onion); use the third for the curry base.
ingredients.notes: Fry 2 onions into golden beresta (fried onion); use the third for the curry base.

ingredients.name: plain curd (yogurt)
visual_checks: Used for marinating; tenderises the meat and adds a subtle tang.
ingredients.notes: Used for marinating; tenderises the meat and adds a subtle tang.

subs.name: 3 tbsp thick coconut milk for a dairy-free version — the tenderness is slightly reduced but still good.

ingredients.name: ghee
visual_checks: Added partway through for richness; do not substitute with oil for the full effect.
ingredients.notes: Added partway through for richness; do not substitute with oil for the full effect.

subs.name: Equal amount of butter provides a similar richness; neutral oil works if keeping it fully dairy-free.

ingredients.name: garam masala
visual_checks: Half added during cooking, half sprinkled at the end.
ingredients.notes: Half added during cooking, half sprinkled at the end.

steps.text: Toss the chicken pieces with curd, 1 tbsp ginger-garlic paste, ¼ tsp turmeric, and a generous pinch of salt. Cover and marinate for at least 15 minutes, or up to 4 hours in the refrigerator for maximum tenderness.

Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium-high heat. Add the sliced onions from two of the onions and fry, stirring regularly, for 18–20 minutes until they are a very deep golden brown and crispy at the edges. Remove half and set aside as beresta (garnish); leave the other half in the pot.

To the remaining fried onion in the pot, add the bay leaves, cinnamon stick, cardamom pods, and cloves. Stir for 30 seconds. Add the third sliced raw onion and the remaining 1 tbsp ginger-garlic paste; cook for 3 minutes until the raw onion softens.

Add the remaining turmeric, red chilli powder, coriander powder, and half the garam masala. Stir vigorously for 2 minutes, adding a splash of water if needed to prevent sticking, until the masala is fragrant and the oil begins to separate around the edges.

Add the marinated chicken pieces and increase the heat to high. Stir and fry for 8–10 minutes, turning the pieces frequently, until the chicken is no longer pink on the outside and has developed colour. This high-heat bhuno (frying) step is what gives the roast its characteristic depth.

Add 200 ml water, stir, and bring to a boil. Reduce heat to low, cover, and braise for 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the gravy has thickened. Stir in the ghee and remaining garam masala, cook uncovered for 2 more minutes. Scatter the reserved beresta on top before serving.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: curd → 3 tbsp thick coconut milk for a dairy-free version — the tenderness is slightly reduced but still good.; ghee → Equal amount of butter provides a similar richness; neutral oil works if keeping it fully dairy-free.; chicken → 800 g bone-in mutton pieces — increase the braising time to 40 minutes and add 100 ml extra water.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook in a thick-bottomed kadai or a degchi; a pressure cooker can be used for the braising stage (2 whistles on medium) but reduces the bhuno character of the dish. The technique is similar to North Indian murgh masala. Ghee and whole spices are pantry staples in Indian kitchens. The key distinguishing element is the very deep caramelisation of onions — do not rush this step.
